issue_title: It would be nice if gabbi.reporter had some test coverage
issue_body:
Not only because it probably just should, but also because it is blowing the stats. But low priority in any case.

Note that I'm just saying reporter here, not the runner, although the same techniques (mentioned below) could be used to check it.

It seems like the most straightforward way to make it happens is to

* create some yaml with one test for each outcome mode and feed it to `driver.test_suite_from_yaml` and get a `suite`
* make sys.stdout a six.StringIO
* run ConciseTestRunner with the `suite`
* inspect the StringIO

It could also be done with mocks but I'd rather not see them in gabbi anywhere.

issue_title: PyPI source tar.gz fails to build
issue_body:
Installing from wheel works fine, but installing via the source tar.gz (via `pip install tulipy`) fails.

1. Dependencies must not be correctly specified, as it throws errors during setup about `numpy` or `Cython` missing instead of installing them before attempting setup.
2. Contents of `libindicators` directory is not included in the package, as compilation failes (e.g. `fatal error: 'indicators.h' file not found`).

Looks like the `Faker` namespace on Pypi has been abandoned for quite a while: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/Faker

This project's owner @joke2k should file a request to have the namespace transferred to this project. 

It would reduce confusion between Faker (project name) and `fake-factory` (how it gets pip installed). 

The pypi team is pretty good about transferring namespaces if they're clearly abandoned (which seems to be the case here).

issue_title: Deviations and DSDL plugin may not play well together
issue_body:
```
I believe I've found a bug related to deviations within pyang DSDL plugin. 
Deviations allow you to reference targets via absolute-schema-node-id which 
means that it can reference anything in the expanded children of a statement 
(one of the entries within Statement.i_children in pyang terms).

For some reason dsdl.py avoids using Statement.i_children all together. It has 
it's own “expansion phases” which take care of augmentations, refinements 
and grouping usages. Yet these fail to take deviations into account.

If I want to deviate a schema node which appears within the original statement 
structure of a module then it's probably going to be okay if I generate DSDL 
based on the deviated module. Pyang validation will simply change the original 
statement tree by applying the deviation. However, if I want to deviate a 
schema node which gets added to the schema tree via a grouping usage for 
example, then I'm going to have a bad time. The only place where the deviation 
will take effect is within Statement.i_children which never gets used within 
dsdl.py.

I've enclosed two sample modules which demonstrate the bug. One defines a 
schema tree and the other applies deviations to it. I used type-stmt replacing 
but this bug applies to any deviate-stmt which targets “virtual schema 
nodes”. If you use pyang to generate DSDL with these two modules, then the 
first deviation will fail to replace the type within hybrid schema and the 
second will succeed.

You should apply deviations the same way you do this for augmentation, 
refinement and grouping usage. The problem is that the original statement tree 
may have already been changed through deviations during module validation and 
you have no way of knowing that. Or you should somehow check if a statement has 
been deviated and create a new rng:define for it, instead of rng:ref-using the 
rng:define for a grouping, etc. Either way you cannot avoid using 
Statement.i_children.

I hope I succeeded in describing the problem. Deviations suck! :)

P.S.: the enclosed module which defines the schema tree has a dependency. 
```

Original issue reported on code.google.com by `jernej.t...@gmail.com` on 12 Sep 2012 at 11:25

Attachments:
* [sample-interfaces@2012-09-12.yang](https://storage.googleapis.com/google-code-attachments/pyang/issue-77/comment-0/sample-interfaces@2012-09-12.yang)
* [sample-interfaces-devs@2012-09-12.yang](https://storage.googleapis.com/google-code-attachments/pyang/issue-77/comment-0/sample-interfaces-devs@2012-09-12.yang)

issue_title: xml to json conversion using pyang & xsltproc
pyvera throwing exception error on dimmer state refresh
issue_body:
Not sure if this is only happening to me or to anyone else as well? 
Maybe not many people are using Vera and HomeAssistant?

Under the get_brightness def, line 577 of the __init__.py file, there is a reference to the **_level_** variable as an integer **_(int(level))_**. 

When refreshing the state of my dimmers from Vera, they return a % value (e.g. 20%) which causes an error in parsing the __init__py file as the 'level' variable is not an integer in that case but a string.

This generally happens every 2 or 3 days. Not sure why it occurs that infrequently.
I believe this has happened with all versions I've used so far - v0.39.x up to current v0.44.2 but I didn't notice earlier as I was always restarting Home Assistant while I was playing with it/setting things up. I've been using it for just over a month now.
I only really noticed this issue as I am graphing temperatures in grafana/InfluxDB and after this error, the temperature sensors in Vera stop updating in Home Assistant and therefore the graphs are blank.

I have implemented a workaround that forces the level variable to an integer if it is not already.
I am sure there are more elegant ways to get the same result?

I have added the following line to my local version today (line 577) above where the error normally occurs. This should strip the % character and change **_level_** to an integer if it is not already one.

**_level = int(level) if type(level) == int else float(level.strip('%'))_**

Full section (including extra line) is as follows (from line 565):

    def get_brightness(self, refresh=False):
        """Get dimmer brightness.

        Refresh data from Vera if refresh is True, otherwise use local cache.
        Refresh is only needed if you're not using subscriptions.
        Converts the Vera level property for dimmable lights from a percentage
        to the 0 - 255 scale used by HA.
        """
        if refresh:
            self.refresh()
        brightness = 0
        level = self.get_value('level')
        level = int(level) if type(level) == int else float(level.strip('%'))
        percent = 0 if level is None else int(level)
        if percent > 0:
            brightness = round(percent * 2.55)
        return int(brightness)


Will report back in 2 days if that hasn't resolved my issue.
If it does, can we get something similar added into the main branch?

Thanks,
Alan

issue_title: Exhaustive Server Pool is too exhaustive
issue_body:
When using a server pool with `active=True` and `exhaust=True`, if an LDAP server goes offline, it is never able to be used again. In a case where there may only be 2 servers in the pool, this is really detrimental to a stateful app, and requires the app to be restarted in order to re-negotiate connections to these LDAP servers. 

To try combat this, I have attempted to use exhaust=False. With the server offline, I attempt to make an LDAP connection, however the pool ends up in a loop attempting to find an active server. The loop never breaks when the server comes back online, and leads to an endless loop of polling.

issue_title: Issues when converting feature into BED12
issue_body:
Hi there,

We (me and @aemoor) recently ran into issues when converting `feature` into `BED12`

The error was from the function `FeatureDB.bed12`. When provided a mouse gtf file from Ensembl, the below error message occurred.

> `File "/Users/andreas/miniconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/gffutils/interface.py", line 1192, in bed12
% (last, feature.stop))
ValueError: End of last exon (91950640) does not match end of feature (91950725)

It seems like [line 1215-1217](https://github.com/daler/gffutils/blob/master/gffutils/interface.py#L1215-L1217) in `interface.py` is trying to validate whether the ends of the exon and the feature. Is this absolutely necessary? Is there a way to get around this? Thanks.

Han

```python
from scapy.all import *
```

It seems that medium to large projects cause problems on RTD.

- [ ] Stop colorizing output on RTD, they don't let you have color (:rage:)
- [ ] Suppress doxygen output with `/dev/null` on RTD

The hacky `silent` branch verified that [exhale is the cause of `cat conf.py` being the last step before failure](https://github.com/rtfd/readthedocs.org/issues/3580).

If anybody ever reads this, one way to test `exhale` is generate the full api and track it on git (you can force-push this away, or do it on a different branch altogether and kill that branch).

Suppose you got

- docs/
    - api/
        - library_root.rst and all the others
    - doxyoutput/
    - conf.py

Then you want to locally `make html` once.  Now `git add -Af api/ doxyoutput` so that all the generated reStructuredText and doxygen files are going to be available in a `clone`.  Now edit `conf.py` and comment out the `exhale` entry in `extensions` and anything else `exhale` related (don't forget to `git add conf.py` changes).

Hopefully you shouldn't need to do this, but it can help identify where the problem is.  In this case, doing that produced a successful build, so clearly `exhale` is the problem!

When a ran autopep8 over a large codebase, I noticed one file that was causing it to crash.

It seems to only happen in specific cases. This is what I had to have in my Python file in to get it to trigger:
1. A function
1. A single empty line
1. A long comment (long enough to trigger E501, 80 characters in my case)
1. A variable assignment

```python
def foo(bar):

#This is a long comment. It has more than 80 characters in it. It triggers an E501
baz = []
```

issue_title: Add -V CLI argument for display current sshtunnel version

issue_body:
Hi,

When reading an image stack with `libtiff.TIFF` I often get the following warning message: 

```
TIFFReadDirectory: Warning, Unknown field with tag 32781 (0x800d) encountered.
TIFFFetchNormalTag: Warning, ASCII value for tag "ImageDescription" does not end in null byte.
```

I have read ([here](http://public.kitware.com/pipermail/insight-users/2007-August/023376.html)) that the original `libtiff` library provides a way to disable them through the option `TIFFSetWarningHandler(0)`, however, I cannot find the option in pylibtiff. Is this option available?

If not, this option would really be helpful: when loading a movie of 3D pictures, it frequently happens that I get thousands of warning that overflow my console.
Thank you

I've been working on a python library that generates an MDF file from scratch, given the inputs for channel groups. I'm noticing that when I use Vector's MDF Validator tool I'm not getting any errors and all of my channel groups are recognized just fine. I am, however, producing _unsorted_ MDFs and am wondering if mdfreader only handles _sorted_ files?

When I try to upload an MDF using your package I am seeing a **KeyError: 'data'** error. [https://imgur.com/a/Bjapk](url) <-- Screenshot of console

Any ideas what this might be?

Hi,

I have mdf files with a size in GB and need lazy access to raw values and the calculated ones.

I know I can access the calculated ones using the `noDataLoading = True` flag but I have to open the file with `convertAfterRead=False` to access the raw values. Since I want to process the mdf file channel by channel I'll end up reading the file once again each time I need access to a channel's the raw values...

Could you add the possibility to provide raw values on demand?

Actually you add the `@protected` and `@private` commands if the function has an single underscore `_` respectively double underscore `__`.
The interpretation of the underscores is correct but [doxygen will ignore them](http://www.stack.nl/~dimitri/doxygen/manual/commands.html#cmdprotected) if the language is object-oriented:

> \protected
> Indicates that the member documented by the comment block is protected, i.e., should only be accessed by other members in the same or derived classes.
>
> Note that Doxygen automatically detects the protection level of members in object-oriented languages. This command is intended for use only when the language does not support the concept of protection level natively (e.g. C, PHP 4).

(same for private)

doxygen actually mark everything as private which begins with one or more underscore.
Of course you can nothing do to change that, I want to only leave this information here for users which are confused (and that you do not have to add the `@protected` and `@private` commands).
I opened an [issue](https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=748753) so that it will be corrected.

The requirement of root access won't work for a large number of applications.

I didn't look through what made that a requirement, but it looks like `ensure_root` is littered all over the code base for a posix environment.

issue_title: SI convention is for a (non-breaking) space between the quantity and the symbol
issue_body:
See item #15 in this checklist:

[](http://physics.nist.gov/cuu/Units/checklist.html)

"There is a space between the numerical value and unit symbol, even when the value is used in an adjectival sense, except in the case of superscript units for plane angle."

issue_title: Python crashes after jedi imports similar C modules in one Python module
issue_body:
The steps to find this issue is firstly reported [here.](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python/issues/817)

In summary, [vscode-python](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python) uses an external Python process and jedi to provide, e.g., function parameter information of Python source files. However, when vscode-python submits to jedi a Python module file (.py) which contains two or more *similar* C modules ```import```s, jedi will import all C modules and then crashes the external Python process. Here is an example file having such issue: [_import_c_extension.py](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/master/caffe2/python/_import_c_extension.py)

When vscode-python submits this file *_import_c_extension.py* to jedi, jedi imports both ```from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu import *``` and ```from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state import *``` to the external Python process. However, the C module ```caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu``` has similar objects (e.g., functions) as ```caffe2_pybind11_state```. They differ only in C function implementations: one for CPU only machine, and another for GPU-capable machine. Importing both C modules crashes the external Python process. Actually, I can reproduce the Python crash directly in terminal:
```
[myh@dsrl2 detectron]$ python2
Python 2.7.5 (default, Aug  4 2017, 00:39:18) 
[GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-16)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state import *
>>> from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu import *
Key already registered.
Offending key: Python.
(crash)
[myh@dsrl2 detectron]$ 
```
This should be not a bug of [_import_c_extension.py](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/master/caffe2/python/_import_c_extension.py), because it conditionally imports only one of ```caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu``` and ```caffe2_pybind11_state```. vscode-python developers think the crash is an issue of jedi. See the [discussion.](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python/issues/817) Isn't it?

Additionally, for reproducing this bug, one might need to build [caffe2](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2) (with GPU enabled) (commit: 5651c4ce0279c895cbb1d1eeecc6594cc8791103) with GPU support (requiring a CUDA-capable graphics card) from source. Or writing a minimal set of C modules...

I've found out that it's way more convenient, rather than applying even popular colorschemes and themes, such as Solarised, Zenburn, etc. to just `wal` away instead of finding the correct theme for each of my programs. 

My point is: should we put all of these together and have a repo of popular schemes for wal?

issue_title: Consumer blocks indefinitely when calling poll() with eventlet
issue_body:
We have a long running consumer process that consumes messages from a single partition kafka topic. There are 3 consumer instances in the group. About 1-2 times every day (these run for days at a time) all 3 consumer instances will all block within 2 minutes of one another. They block when they all call into poll(). A monitoring process restarts them after 10 minutes (after detecting they are blocking), and then the group will begin consuming again and catching up on old messages.

We call into poll with a timeout of 30000 ms. I understand that poll() will block on a rebalance but it seems like this is not the sole cause of a 10 minute block.

Here are some of the logs from what I found.

The one below is logged when the consumer instance has been detected to be blocked for 10 minutes. 
![screen shot 2016-10-20 at 8 49 14 am](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/7716924/19563910/57f3d676-96a7-11e6-8036-1d9b02aeb99f.png)

Then after doing some more analysis, I saw that it was stopping after calling poll with the timeout of 30000 ms. I then ran a stack tracer in the consumer process to print out the stacks of all the threads in the process. Below is the trace of the thread that is actually consuming messages from kafka.
![screen shot 2016-10-20 at 8 36 56 am](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/7716924/19564222/60db7c0c-96a8-11e6-97df-d64e4b5f189c.png)

Doing some more digging I actually see the partition get revoked
![screen shot 2016-10-20 at 9 52 39 am](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/7716924/19565233/c42355f2-96ab-11e6-8366-98460ce02d29.png)


![screen shot 2016-10-20 at 9 59 47 am](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/7716924/19565505/adb187b6-96ac-11e6-9597-7947f75fee4e.png)


![screen shot 2016-10-20 at 10 06 09 am](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/7716924/19565575/e31f032e-96ac-11e6-9a1e-d6432c924857.png)

It seems like the partition is changed in two instances but the last log that is seen in instance 0 is just the poll (never sees a partition change). 

Any help/ advice is greatly appreciated. I described our workflow in this previous issue
https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/issues/842

Reading the description of https://pypi.org/project/selectors2/, it looks like it adds some additional features beyond https://pypi.org/project/selectors34/

In particular, retrying system interrupts (PEP 475) would be useful.

I have not explored this in depth, so opening this ticket as a placeholder for now.

```
    self._next_partition_records = self._parse_fetched_data(completion)
```

Feature request/suggestion... 

- We should be able `query.count()` any model that has a unique field. It's just a question of finding the right column (...`uidx`), and doing an `HLEN` on the hash for that column.

- Similarly we should be able to iterate over all members in any model, using the newer (v 2.8) [HSCAN](http://redis.io/commands/hscan) redis command. The order of results would be random and some items maybe missed if the table changes during operation, but it would still be useful.

- Even for models with no index and no unique fields, we can still use [SCAN](http://redis.io/commands/scan) to count and index records. A runtime warning about poor performance/bad design might be in order, but it can still work.

Bottom line: I'd really like to see `Model.count()` and `for i in Model` just work. 

(BTW: Big fan of ROM.)

issue_title: User interface quicker. Too many clicks per annotation.
issue_body:
Hi,
I am using this tools to distribute it to students so they can annotate images. We have been trying it for a while, in UBUNTU 16.04 machines and it is a very simple and straight forward tool. Perfect for the job.

However, our students claim that they need too many mouse clicks per image, and after evaluating the user interface and usability, we have come to the conclusion that the label image tool would need an improvement in the usability.

At the moment, a whole annotation requires:

Next Image button (1 click) -> Create Box button (1 click)->  actual box creation (2 clicks) -> Select label dialogue (2 fast clicks) -> Save button (1 click) -> Enter (1 touch key) ->

Next Image ... -> ...

That is 7 clicks plus 1 enter key.

Our request (as we dont have the knowledge or resources to accomplish it) would be to reduce it to 2 clicks.

That would be, once the image to work with is on the screen: 

- 1st click: Selecting the first corner of bounding box (xmin, ymin)
- 2nd click: Selecting the second corner of bounding box (xmax, ymax), select automatically the label (the previous selected one), save it with the recommended name in the previous location(name of image.xml) with no dialogue box, then go to next image and activate the Create box tool, signaling the image as a non edited one (not to confuse the operator), as after half an hour, the operator start to get confused with the images, so it would be perfect a visual signal to see if the image is being edited.

That way, we reduce the work form 8 interactions to 2. Obviously, that would be implemented with a mode that user could select (turbo mode), so the user can always go back to the normal mode, to change the label, location..or any other setting.

Well, that is our result of a studding after using it for a while.

Thanks for the good work.

Alex

I love this solution, but I am somewhat of a JS newbie. I have 2 questions.

This is working properly, but the Portfolio column (first column) filter is still showing all possible selections even though the column items have been reduced by the first filter. For example: With In Process selected the Portfolio filter should only show one choice.

If I select "Completed" then hide the Schedule column and show the Budget column. A little background..the table will eventually have 30 or so columns with some being hidden/shown based on the Project Status drop down menu.

issue_title: error <my_config.h> MUST be included first - CentOS 7
issue_body:
Hi,

The newest version of mariadb-devel (mariadb-devel-5.5.40-1.el7_0.x86_64) on CentOS 7 breaks when installing MySQL-python via pip:

In file included from /usr/include/mysql/my_config.h:14:0,

                 from _mysql.c:44:

/usr/include/mysql/my_config_x86_64.h:654:2: error: #error <my_config.h> MUST be included first!

 #error <my_config.h> MUST be included first!

  ^

error: command 'gcc' failed with exit status 1


I resolved this by removing all mariadb packages via yum:

yum remove maria*

Then installing an older version (mariadb-devel-5.5.37-1.el7_0.x86_64). This seemed to fix the issue.

Hope this can help anyone else in the same situation.

issue_title: Add key and serializer-class-method support to associations
Multiple Directories not working in Windows with Py3 and PyMinifier 2.1
issue_body:
Apparently, Wildcards -as shown in the docs- are not being supported on Windows.

Example: `pyminifier -d "C:\Users\admin\Desktop\Projects\Python 3.5.1\sysAuth\New" "C:\Users\user\Desktop\Projects\abc\xyz\*.py"` gives error `OSError: [WinError 123] The filename, directory name, or volume label syntax is incorrect: 'C:\\Users\\admin\\Desktop\\Projects\\Python 3.5.1\\sysAuth\\*.py'`.

However, when a definite filename in the directory is give, like `"C:\Users\user\Desktop\Projects\abc\xyz\helloWorld.py"`, PyMinifier works perfectly.

This is not a problem with Linux, but for compatibility, I think a different method for going through directories should be used.

Code in [visualize_cam](https://github.com/raghakot/keras-vis/blob/master/vis/visualization.py#L173) may perform a division by zero.

Assume we want to visualize the class 'cat' but `seed_img` is an image of a 'dog'. Hence, it is likely that the heatmap (weighted sum of the feature maps) is all negative (since `seed_img` does not contain any 'cat' features). In [line 272](https://github.com/raghakot/keras-vis/blob/master/vis/visualization.py#L272), we threshold the heatmap at 0. This produces an array containing only the value 0. In [line 273](https://github.com/raghakot/keras-vis/blob/master/vis/visualization.py#L273), we divide by the maximum of this array:
`heatmap /= np.max(heatmap)`
Hence, we would divide by zero. 

Two suggestions to solve this issue:
1. `heatmap /= np.max(heatmap) + epsilon`
2. `if np.max(heatmap) > 0: heatmap /= np.max(heatmap)`

At the moment, if a code file's accompanying XML file is changed, a re-parse is triggered and the code base is considered altered, _even_ if the code file itself didn't change. This also triggers a re-run of all the unit tests that depend on that code file.

If the only thing that changed is the unit test specification for a specific subroutine, then _only_ that subroutine's unit tests should be re-run. We could do this by comparing the new XML contents to the old ones and then recognize what actually changed.

issue_title: mlaunch should print error message for invalid options, rather than silently ignoring
issue_body:
Currently, `mlaunch` will silently ignore any invalid options that you attempt to pass through. For example, the following will not produce any syntax errors:
```
mlaunch init --single --carrots --apples
```

I understand that we will need to check both `mongos` and `mongod` for what is a valid option - however, it would be nice to error out for invalid syntax - otherwise a user may assume something works (e.g. setting storage engine to WiredTiger) when they actually mis-spelt it, and it did not work.

return super(cls, cls).NewFromJsonDict(data=data,

Below is my debug output. 
I believe the request for the data address using FC2 is calculated incorrectly.
I am new to this depth of modbus, but I believe FC2 itself implies a compensation of a +10001 offset of the address parameter given to it. 

My FC2 request to the slave d'01', register d'100001' populates a data address of x'27 11' (d 10001).
I believe the request packet should be:
(01 02 **_00 00_** 00 01 xx xx)
 as compared to
(01 02 27 11 00 01 xx xx)
```
MinimalModbus debug mode. Writing to instrument (expecting 6 bytes back): "\x01\x02'\x11\x00\x01\xe3{" (01 02 27 11 00 01 E3 7B)
MinimalModbus debug mode. No sleep required before write. Time since previous read: 1469204431578.1 ms, minimum silent period: 4.01 ms.
MinimalModbus debug mode. Response from instrument: '\x01\x82\x02\xc1a' (01 82 02 C1 61) (5 bytes), roundtrip time: 50.6 ms. Timeout setting: 50.0 ms.
```
It is possible I am misunderstanding the protocol, so any input to confirm/deny would be appreciated. This is the information I am using that has led me to this belief:

> Read Input Status (FC=02)
>
>Request
>
>This command is requesting the ON/OFF status of discrete inputs # 10197 to 10218
>from the slave device with address 17.

>11 02 00C4 0016 BAA9

>11: The Slave Address (11 hex = address17 )
>02: The Function Code 2 (read Input Status)
>00C4: The Data Address of the first input to read.
>             ( 00C4 hex = 196 , + 10001 offset = input #10197 )
>0016: The total number of coils requested. (16 hex = 22,  inputs 197 to 218 )
>BAA9: The CRC (cyclic redundancy check) for error checking

[(http://www.simplymodbus.ca/FC02.htm)]

I have been digging through the code to try and find where this information is parsed to see the method used, but this is my first project in Python and I'm having difficulty navigating the rabbit hole to find the source.

Any response is much appreciated,
Thanks!

Hello, first of all great work, and thanks for sharing your library!

I want to comment on some of the things that are related to error handling in you code. There are a couple of small issues.

1)  At very least it is important to provide `Exception` class to the `except`

```
try:
    ...
except Exception:
    ...
```

If you don't provide `except Exception`, any kind of exception will be handled, including such as `KeyboardInterrupt`. Check https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#exception-hierarchy. If you make an error and spin up an infinite loop it might be difficult to kill it if you handle KeyboarInterrupt, and don't raise any error.

2) In most cases class passed to except should be as concrete as possible. For example in this case:

```
    def avg_letter_per_word(self, text):
        try:
            ALPW = float(float(self.char_count(text))/float(self.lexicon_count(text)))
            return round(ALPW, 2)
        except:
            print("Error(ALPW): Number of words are zero, cannot divide")
            return
```
a number of different things can go wrong inside `try ... except`. However In any case it just spits out `Number of words are zero`. This will make it very hard to debug issues. Much better would be:
```
try:
   ...
except ZeroDivisionError:
   ...
```

3) Instead of `print()` much better way to report errors is `raise Exception('... message ...)`, ideally with a custom exception class derived in your code.

issue_title: DataLoader batch_load_fn not batching in graphene (but works without graphene)
issue_body:
This test case doesn't appear to work when using graphene:

https://github.com/syrusakbary/promise/blob/master/tests/test_dataloader.py#L32

The batch load function is called with one key at a time when calling `dataloader.load_many([6, 7])`. I wasn't able to make that test case break by running it many times or with large (100,000) array sizes, which is why I suspect it's due to an interaction with graphene. It works in `v2.0.2` (the `pip install promise` version), but not `promise/master`.

Here is roughly the code that I'm running:

```
class HeroLoader(DataLoader):
    def batch_load_fn(self, friend_ids):
        import logging; logging.error(friend_ids)  ########### Console log #2 here
        url = '{}/?id={}'.format(
            Hero.endpoint,
            ','.join([str(id) for id in friend_ids])
        )
        response = requests.get(url)
        results = response.json()['results']
        return Promise.resolve(results)

        
class Hero(graphene.ObjectType):
    endpoint = '{}/heroes'.format(HOST)
    data_loader = HeroLoader()

    id = graphene.Int()
    name = graphene.String(name='name')
    friend_ids = graphene.List(graphene.Int)
    friends = graphene.List(partial(lambda: Hero))

    def resolve_friends(self, args, context, info):
        import logging; logging.error(self.friend_ids)           ######### Console log #1 here
        heroes_json = self.data_loader.load_many(self.friend_ids)
        return heroes_json.then(do_stuff_with_promise......)
```

The console output of an infringing request looks like this (and stepping through with a debugger yields the same results):

```
ERROR:root:[6, 7]
ERROR:root:[6]
ERROR:root:[7]
```

The expected output is
```
ERROR:root:[6, 7]
ERROR:root:[6, 7]
```

If you want a test that works with one version of promise, but not the other:

* Use this repo and branch: https://github.com/curiousest/graphql-to-rest/tree/failing-promises
* `py.test --capture=no -k 'test_batch_requests' -s `

issue_title: Create a merge tool for the .hypothesis directory
issue_body:
I've been using hypothesis for a while now, and have become so dependent on it that I use it on every machine I've got access to.  This is both good and bad; I now have .hypothesis directories on several machines, each of which have different sets of failing examples.  That means that to be sure I've killed a bug, I need to run the same code on multiple different machines, simply because I don't have an easy way of merging the various directories, with their associated examples, into one master directory.  Can you create a tool that can merge multiple .hypothesis directories into one?

Ideally, this would be a tool that is git-compatible.  That is, something where the following works:

> git config --global merge.tool customMergeTool
> git config --global mergetool.customMergeTool.cmd 'customMergeTool.sh \"$BASE\" \"$LOCAL\" \"$REMOTE\" \"$MERGED\"'

See http://stackoverflow.com/questions/23313620/select-git-mergetool-based-on-file-type for more information about this.

The reason I want it to also be git compatible is because I want to commit the .hypothesis directory to my repository; there are cases where something fails, but only VERY rarely.  If we lose that one case, then it will be hard if not impossible to recreate it.  By having the ability to store the directory in the repository, not only do we protect against the loss of a machine with a valuable example, I and my coworkers immediately get access to the failing example, and can independently test our code.  Finally, our build/test farm also gets it, and can do repeated nightly tests, blindly committing the .hypothesis directory, which we can then all pull from the next day to see if there are any new failing examples.

**References:**
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/23313620/select-git-mergetool-based-on-file-type
http://git-scm.com/docs/git-mergetool
http://git-scm.com/docs/git-config search for `mergetool.<tool>.path` and `mergetool.<tool>.cmd`

I'd like to add support for a `__pretty_str__` (or similar) protocol that hypothesis can use when printing falsifying examples. I have some generated data where my inputs can get quite large and complex, and usually I have to copy/paste into vim and manually format the results. The pretty printing wouldn't be appropriate for `__str__` or `__repr__` because those often show up in logger statements and can be shortened, whereas for the test failure I'd like to see more detailed/formatted results.

Would a PR adding this behavior be accepted? If not, is there any plugin system or protocol I can tap into (with a pytest plugin?) which could be used to maintain the behavior in a modular way? I'd much rather do that than try to maintain a fork if this isn't a change you feel is appropriate for the main project.

> # Hashing Module Level Design
> [...]
> ## Component overview
>
> The Hashing module provides one-way hashing functions. Hashing functions are used to create a fixed-length representation of a block of data so that when the data changes the hash value does not match. The hash value is also known as a (message) digest.
> 
> A hashing function is generally used for creating a hash message authentication code (HMAC) when sending a message. Such a HMAC can be used in combination with a previously exchanged symmetric key as a message integrity and authentication control.
> 
> With this module you can thus:
>
> -    Create a hash value/message digest for a file, a stream or a buffer.
> -    Create a HMAC for a stream or a buffer.

issue_title: Repetitive merge_range to same range causes warning in Excel 2016
issue_body:
Hi,

When I, through a program error/bug, repetitively merge_range to the same range, Excel produces two warnings:

1. We found a problem with some content in 'Range.XLSX'. Do you want us to try to recover as much as we can? If you trust the source of this workbook, click Yes. Clicking Yes yields:
2. Excel was able to open the file by repairing or removing the unreadable content. Removed records: Merge cells from /xl/worksheets/sheet2.xml part

I am using Python version 3.4.3 and XlsxWriter  0.8.6 and Excel 2016.

Here is some code that demonstrates the problem:

`    import xlsxwriter

    workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('range.xlsx')
    worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

    merge_format = workbook.add_format({
        'bold': True,
        'align': 'center'
        })

    headings = (
        ("Alpha",   "L1:W1"),
        ("Bravo",   "X1:AI1"),
        ("Charlie", "AJ1:AU1"),
        ("Delta",   "AV1:BG1"))

    for (product, coordinates) in headings:
        worksheet.merge_range("L1:W1", product, merge_format)
`
Granted it was my program error that caused the merge_format at the same coordinate. My guess is that all four merge_range functions were committed to the spreadsheet and when Excel saw all four merge_formats on the same range, Excel threw its hands up. In correcting/recovering the spreadsheet, Excel used the last value, Delta, in the range.

What I did not have time to test to see if a similar error occurs if I try to write to the same cell repetitively. Hope to follow up with a similar test in a couple of days.

issue_title: Drill Authentication and Security with PyDrill
issue_body:
Hello, I am attempting to use PyDrill, and looking through the code, it appears there is an option to pass http basic auth to pydrill, however, Apache Drill does not use http basic auth, it uses a form based mechanism.  

I really like what pydrill gets long term, and would love the ability to add in the auth component.  

For example, I tried the http_auth (see below), and when I did that, I got an unknown mime error. Based on serializer.py, it's not handling text/html, which if I had to guess, is the mime type returned if the login screen is returned. (I.e. if you submit a query without first authenticating, it shows the login screen which is text/html... I would imaging we would want to handle that at some point. 

So, when I use python requests, to auth to drill, I create a session object and keep it open, with the first thing I do is authenticate.  I am not a great python programmer, so please bear with my simplistic caveman like code. 

But basically, I have create a session object that gets passed around, and the first I do is pass it to the authentication function which makes the login request, and the requests module keeps track of my cookies from that point on. Then from there, all request made to drill work properly.   I would like to use pydrill here, because it provides clean options for handling the ssl certs, etc.  But I would like to be able to use the forms based auth like I have in my hacky scripts. Perhaps I just don't see how to do this, but if it's not in the code, I am hoping this helps us get there... 

Thanks!

## Example script that uses authentication
```
#!/usr/bin/python

import requests
import json
import sys
import os

drill_base_url = "https://drillbit.local:8047"

drill_user = "username"
drill_pass = "password"

print "Drill Rest Endpoint: %s" % (drill_base_url)

def main():
    s = requests.Session() # Create a session object
    s = authDrill(s)        # Authenticate to Drill

    r = runQuery(s, "ALTER SESSION set `store.json.all_text_mode` = true")
    result = runQuery(s, "show files")

    print r.text
    
    if result.status_code == 200:
        print result.text
    else:
        print "Error encountered: %s" % result.status_code

def runQuery(s, drill):
    url = drill_base_url + "/query.json"   
    payload = {"queryType":"SQL", "query":drill}
    headers = {"Content-type": "application/json"}
    r = s.post(url, data=json.dumps(payload), headers=headers, verify=False)

    return r
def authDrill(s):

    url = drill_base_url + "/j_security_check"

    login = {'j_username': drill_user, 'j_password': drill_pass}
   
    r = s.post(url, data=login, verify=False)

    if r.status_code == 200:
        if r.text.find("Invalid username/password credentials") >= 0:
            print "Authentication Failed - Please check Secrets - Exiting"
            sys.exit(1)
        elif r.text.find("Number of Drill Bits") >= 0:
            print "Authentication successful"
        else:
            print "Unknown Response Code 200 - Exiting"
            print r.text
            sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print "Non HTTP-200 returned - Unknown Error - Exiting"
        print "HTTP Code: %s" % r.status_code
        print r.text
        sys.exit(1)

    return s

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()




```

issue_title: zero lenght closing lines after path parsing

issue_title: Session protection triggering unnecessarily, causing "remember cookie" to be wrongfully cleared when in "strong" mode
issue_body:
I've been working through Miguel Grinberg's "Flask Web Development" book, which makes use of Flask-Login. I'm using version v0.3.2. The book's example project sets the session protection to "strong" mode and utilizes the "remember me" feature. I noticed that my login session is only remembered on the very first request upon returning to the site after closing my browser then re-opening it. On the second request, my login session is wiped out. I dug through the code to help me see what was going on. Here's what I came up with:

1. User opens browser, navigates to site
2. User logs in, with "remember me" enabled
3. session['_id'] is initialized with call to _create_identifier()
4. The "remember cookie" is created
5. User closes browser, while still logged in
6. User opens browser, navigates to site, expecting their session to still be active
7. User info is loaded from the "remember cookie" on the first request (gives the user the impression they're still logged in and everything is fine)
8. On the very next request, however, session protection is wrongfully triggered due to there no longer being a session['_id'] value (therefore it doesn't match the per-request-generated challenge value)
9. Since session protection is triggered while the mode is "strong", all of the SESSION_KEYS are popped from the session and the "remember cookie" is cleared
10. The user is now logged out

The issue is not quite as bad in "basic" mode. Session protection is still wrongfully triggered, on all requests after the first one, due to the missing session['_id'] value, but the only side effect is that session['_fresh'] is set to False (which is the value it already would have anyway as a result of a previous call to _load_from_cookie()). The user still remains logged in after closing and re-opening the browser in "basic" mode.

I experimented with the code and came up with an idea that seemed to accomplish my goal. I altered _set_cookie() and _load_from_cookie() in such a way that the "remember cookie" stores session['_id'] along with session['user_id']. With this method in place, session protection is not wrongfully triggered (see step 8 above), since session['_id'] was loaded in from the "remember cookie." I'm not sure if this is the best long-term solution or if it's a potential security issue. It seems that if the cookie ended up in the wrong hands, session protection would kick in and drop the session when it compares the _id value from the cookie to the newly generated one which uses the attacker's IP and user agent string.

issue_title: How to further evolve a network?
issue_body:
After looking through the issues I have seen that people have asked how to save and load networks which can be done with pickle. However, I am interested in further evolving a network that has already been saved. For example, I am using neat to solve a trajectory optimization problem which runs for 1 cycle (8 seconds). I evolved a network to solve this problem over 10,000 generations and saved the best network. Now I want to continue evolving the same network but I want to simulate it for 2 cycles (16 seconds).

If the cycles part is not possible I'm still interested in being able to continue evolving a saved network. the problem with the checkpoint method is that it doesn't save the best network, it just saves the ith network which is usually very lousy compared to the best one.

issue_title: datetime objects used are not timezone aware
issue_body:
The `datetime.utcnow()` function is used anywhere the current datetime is needed. That function returns an offset-naive datetime object by design in [Python 2](https://docs.python.org/2/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.utcnow) and [Python 3](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.utcnow).

This causes problems when using the `arrow` library for dates and times using e.g. SQLAlchemy with [`sqlalchemy_utils`](http://sqlalchemy-utils.readthedocs.org/en/latest/data_types.html#sqlalchemy_utils.types.arrow.ArrowType) installed.

Logging in after the first time fails because for some reason the dates are compared by sqlalchemy before committing the transaction, resulting in the following exception:

    TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes

If the datetime objects were timezone aware, there would not be a problem. And I think some database backends do store this information, so it might be better in those cases too. (I'm using MySQL, so it does not affect me in this regard)

issue_title: Use os.replace instead of os.rename for python 3.3+
issue_body:
I'm using this lib on Windows 10 with no issues so far, but I'm affraid os.rename, though not well documented for Windows, isn't atomic. As per the documentation:

> If you want cross-platform overwriting of the destination, use replace().
https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.rename

So I suggest something along the lines of:

```
def _replace_atomic(src, dst):
        try:
                os.replace(src, dst)
        except:
                os.rename(src, dst)
        _sync_directory(os.path.normpath(os.path.dirname(dst)))
```

issue_title: Improve object cleanup when connection fails
issue_body:
When wrapping a socket connection in a try, so an exception can be caught if the connection can't be made because the socket server is unreachable, e.g.
try:
    ws = SocketIO('localhost', 8080, wait_for_connection=False)
except:
    handle_exception()

An uncaught exception occurs: 
Exception ignored in: bound method SocketIO.__del__ of <socketIO_client.SocketIO object at 0x7f9be435efd0

This is because errors raised by __del__ output to stderr and are not caught.
The cause of this looks to be in _close() when halt() is called on _heartbeat_thread This is because there is no _heartbeat_thread for the object as an exception occurred during initialization. 

A suggested solution is to wrap the halt() call in a try block, e.g.
        try:
            self._heartbeat_thread.halt()
        except:
            pass

I haven't made a pull request with this as the solution as the author may not want this behavior, so I wanted to check first

issue_title: AdImageMixin.remote_read doesn't use the right API
issue_body:
In our application, we never set the global/default FacebookAdsApi to avoid concurrency errors. Instead we rely explicitly on managing our own API objects in a thread-safe way.

We've run into the issue of `AdImage.remote_read` always failing. Tracking down the problem we found the root cause in `AdImageMixin.remote_read` that instantiates AdAccount using global API and ignoring the true API passed with the original AdImage object:
https://github.com/facebook/facebook-python-ads-sdk/blob/master/facebookads/adobjects/helpers/adimagemixin.py#L154

Our hack solution was to monkey-patch `AdImage.remote_read` by cloning it and adding a line `account._api = self._api`. However we'd like to report the problem and suggest a stronger support / automated testing for use cases with no global/default API set

issue_body:

issue_title: ORDER BY query quantifies given order by string (which might be an alias) with a table name
issue_body:
**Example**
ORDER BY query using the alias without a join:
```
str(Query.from_(a).select(a.b, fn.Sum(a.c).as_('alias')).groupby(a.c).orderby('alias') )
=> 'SELECT "b",SUM("c") "alias" FROM "a" GROUP BY "c" ORDER BY "alias"'
```
ORDER BY query using the alias with a join:
```
str(Query.from_(a).join(b).on(a.b_id == b.id).select(a.foo, fn.Sum(a.int_column).as_('alias')).groupby(a.foo).orderby('alias') )
=> 'SELECT "a"."foo",SUM("a"."int_column") "alias" FROM "a" JOIN "b" ON "a"."b_id"="b"."id" GROUP BY "a"."foo" ORDER BY "a"."alias"'
```

**Issue**
The second example shows that when using a string in the `orderby` which might be an alias, is quantified with the FROM-table.

**Suggestion**
Don't quantify with the FROM-table in case of a string.
I think it's the coders responsibility to properly quantify, if necessary. The databases would complain for invalid SQL in case of ambiguous column names.

Let me know if there's a reason to quantify it that I don't see.

issue_title: IE: ocasional NoSuchElementException while locating elements
issue_body:
Update(6/25/2013): Newer versions of Se raising NoSuchElementException instead with message "Finding elements returned an unexpected error". Updating the title to reflect that.

When trying to find elements during a page load and possibly a DOM update, WebDriver sometimes raises a InvalidSelectiorException[sic] when the XPath is valid. This is problematic because it causes Wait Until Page Contains Element to fail. It is difficult to sync to a page load triggered by an AJAX call because the location, URL, and title may not necessarily change. One workaround is to use Wait Until Keyword Succeeds with Page Should Contain Element, but this has some drawbacks:
* Browser pop-ups (e.g. JavaScript errors) are hidden because WebDriver dismisses them on any method call and an exception is only raised once (UPDATE: desired capability unexpectedAlertBehaviour can change this behavior only for Selenium >= 2.25)
* Failures take the full timeout to fail

issue_title: Clarify that a Pipfile is a tool to generate Pipfle.lock, and not a build description on its own.
issue_body:
Executable build descriptions are a bad idea. See https://www.reddit.com/r/Python/comments/5e2vci/pipfile_a_new_and_much_better_way_to_declare/da9c2ku/ or uncountable blog posts and articles all over the Internet for very good examples. It should be common knowledge by now, still the same errors are made again and again.

Pipfile has a nice hybrid approach in that it allows developers to *generate* a _static_ `Pipfile.lock` from a dynamic description. Tools can parse the static `Pipfile.lock` and know exactly what to do, while developers can work with the more convenient `Pipfile` and can be as smart or lazy as they want to. This approach might actually work very well.

For this to work, and for others like me to accept this idea, I thing the following point should be stressed out more: Tools work with `Pipfile.lock` exclusively. A build system should never automatically execute a `Pipfile` to generate a missing or outdated `Pipfile.lock`. The `pipfile` module should never be a build requirement.

issue_title: ChoiceField.valid_value() Fails due to Label when filter with query params
issue_body:
I would like to report a weird action when I try to use filtering with DRF 3.4, django-filter 0.13 and django-enumfields 0.1.  
I made a request with url http://url/api/MyModels?test_type=a and the response said "['Select a valid choice. A is not one of the available choices.']".   
It turns out that ChoiceField.valid_value() returns False because [k, v in choices](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/forms/fields.py#L819) are both label instead of name and value so it keeps failing.  
They should be name and value and tries to compare with query param which is 'a'.  
This is my sample code

```pytyon
class TestType(Enum) 
    A = 'a'
    
    class Labels:
        A = 'a-label'

class MyModel(Model):
    test_type = EnumField(TestType, max_length=MAX_TEST_TYPE_LENGTH)
```

I think EnumField should override valid_value() and makes sure overridden method called instead of ChoiceField.

issue_title: auto-upgrade can break when a previous install of a different package *also* auto-upgraded astropy-helpers
issue_body:
This is related to #144, but even more obscure.

For this bug to occur:

1. A package using astropy-helpers (say, WebbPSF) has astropy (or any other package the uses astropy-helpers) as a build-time dependency say, in setup_requires.
2. The top-level package must have an out of date astropy-helpers that automatically upgrades itself, say, from v0.4 to v0.4.8.
3. The secondary package (installed via setup_requires) must also have an out of date astropy-helpers, but of a more recent version generation.  For example, it must have astropy-helpers v1.0 when the latest version in the v1.0.x series is, say, v1.0.1.

When the secondary package tries to upgrade astropy-helpers from v1.0 to v1.0.1 it will fail.  This is because the *previously* upgrade astropy-helpers (v0.4.8) will already be imported and in sys.modules.  In particular the `astropy_helpers.version` module will already be in sys.modules.  astropy-helpers uses this in its setup.py to determine what version string it should use.  So even though the new astropy-helpers has `VERSION = '1.0.1' in its setup.py, it may still end up reporting `version='0.4.8'`, which gets read out of the already imported `astropy_helpers.version` :(

The end result of all this is that astropy-helpers v1.0.1 gets built into a .egg package, but with a filename like `astropy-helpers-0.4.8`.  The easy_install process that is responsible for performing temporary installations of setup_requires packages in turn recognizes this as a failure to fetch/build/install astropy-helpers *v1.0.1*, which it was expecting to find after this process.   Instead it finds something that looks, superficially, like astropy-helpers v0.4.8 and throws up its hands in confusion.

To mitigate this two steps should be taken:

1) Existing imports of `astropy_helpers` and all submodules should be cleaned out of `sys.modules` far earlier in the process of running ah_bootstrap.  The fix in #144 addressed cleaning up old imports of `astropy_helpers`, but too late in the process to affect this specific issue.

2) The `generate_version_py` helper function shouldn't go directly through the import system to get to the `packagename.version` module.  Instead it should open that file and compile it on its own, so that there is no ambiguity as to where it is coming from in any given setup.py run.
In coordination with astropy core 3.0, we can/should remove any python2 related code from here.

issue_title: HOG visualization should use midpoints of bins
issue_body:
## Description
The HOG visualization draws lines that are perpendicular to the edges of orientation bins.  It would be clearer if the lines were perpendicular to the _midpoints_ of bins, instead of the edges.

For example, if `hog` is applied with two orientation bins (`orientations=2`) to the image on the left below (reproducible by the code below), I would have expected the visualization to produce something looking like "concentric diamonds."  But it instead looks like the image to its right.

![comparison](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5924638/21959910/293b0192-da8b-11e6-8ba9-c72c5085a368.png)

If others agree that this would be a worthwhile change, I'd be happy to work on a pull request for it.

## Way to reproduce
Here is the code to reproduce the images above:
```python
import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
from skimage.feature import hog

X,Y = np.mgrid[-256:256,-256:256]
image = np.sqrt(X**2 + Y**2)
_, hog_image = hog(
    image,
    orientations=2,
    pixels_per_cell=(32, 32),
    cells_per_block=(1, 1),
    visualise=True
)
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 8))
ax1.imshow(image, cmap=plt.cm.gray, interpolation='nearest')
ax2.imshow(hog_image, cmap=plt.cm.gray, interpolation='nearest')
```
(I'm using Python 3.5.2, scikit-image 0.12.3)

issue_title: SP Save/load causes duty cycle variations, which cause boost factor variations
issue_body:
The prototest is failing.

~~~
ASSERTION FAILED: "outputBaseline[i] == outputC[i]"
~~~

It works fine for the capnproto `read` / `write`, but it fails for the older `save` /  `load`.

I debugged it, and I saw that serializing and deserializing the SP causes the columns' active duty cycles to start to diverge. Eventually they become large enough to cause a boost factor variation, where some columns have a boost factor of 0.99 and others of 1.01. This causes different columns to get activated.

I don't recommend this change, but the test starts passing when we change this line https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/3e71bb596432d1c3137c02d5c79c7d638efceb66/src/nupic/algorithms/SpatialPooler.cpp#L1156

to

~~~c++
    Real activeDutyCycle = round(activeDutyCycles_[i] * 1000.0) / 1000.0;
    Real boostFactor = exp((targetDensity - activeDutyCycle)* maxBoost_);
~~~

issue_title: onResume deadlock with pyjnius/pygame/sdl on android
issue_body:
It seems that interaction between android.pyx, pyjnius and app pause mode can cause deadlock when going into pause mode or when resuming from pause mode.  I am using the pygame bootstrap.

A few logs and things below to show what's going on

Here's the typical sequence - first I press the home button and the app should pause.

```
I/python  ( 4288): [INFO   ] [Android     ] Must go into sleep mode, check the app
I/python  ( 4288): [INFO   ] [Android     ] App paused, now wait for resume.
I/System.out( 4288): python pyjnius java:invoke(<proxy>, public abstract void android.view.ViewTreeObserver$OnGlobalLayoutListener.onGlobalLayout(), [Ljava.lang.Object;@33d6fa5f)
```

Note that *after* the "app paused" message, the Java interface of pyjnius receives a call which it tries to push to Python.  The log message is from line 15 of NativeInvocationHandler.java of pyjnius

The above happens *before* I try to resume my app.

Once I try to resume, I get a black screen and nothing happens.  I have logging setup on the pygame bootstrap and I can see that the app's onResume() method in PythonActivity.java is never called.

I get the following log output

```
W/ActivityManager( 1005): Activity stop timeout for ActivityRecord{20c40984 u0 org.hottwaj.hottwajtest/org.renpy.android.PythonActivity t8207}
I/ActivityManager( 1005): Activity reported stop, but no longer stopping: ActivityRecord{20c40984 u0 org.hottwaj.hottwajtest/org.renpy.android.PythonActivity t8207}
```

issue_title: @not_on_custom_jira_instance tag not allowing to test on any other instance than pycontribs.atlassian.net
issue_body:
The way the not_on_custom_jira_instance means that any other instance than pycontribs.atlassian.net is considered a custom instance : (https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/ff7fe0b8cd901e6d84abafbc10a5eab405568649/tests/tests.py#L71-L77)
```python
if 'CI_JIRA_URL' in os.environ:
    not_on_custom_jira_instance = pytest.mark.skipif(True, reason="Not applicable for custom JIRA instance")
    logging.info('Picked up custom JIRA engine.')
else:
    def noop(arg):
        return arg
    not_on_custom_jira_instance = noop
```
And that means that a lot of tests are not run when not using a "custom" Jira instance... 
But I would love to run my tests on an instance of Jira on my machine for better speed.

Could someone explain why singling out these tests?

```python
from multiprocessing.pool import ThreadPool
from google.cloud import storage
from functools import partial

def upload(bucket, i):
    blob = bucket.blob("file{}.png".format(i))
    blob.upload_from_string("blabla")
    blob.make_public()
    return blob.public_url

bucket = storage.Client().get_bucket("deepo-test")
pool = ThreadPool()
fct = partial(upload, bucket)
pool.map(fct, [i for i in range(2)])
```

issue_title: BaseARN does not generate valid ARNs for GovCloud and CN partitions of AWS
issue_body:
Gov Cloud and China regions use an alternate partition name for ARNs. The following docs and code from aws which show how to properly build a region-agnostic arn:  [aws arn docs](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/aws-arns-and-namespaces.html#genref-arns)
TL;DR
example arn 

> arn:partition:service:region:account-id:resourcetype:resource

_partition_ : The partition that the resource is in. For standard AWS regions, the partition is aws. If you have resources in other partitions, the partition is aws-partitionname. For example, the partition for resources in the China (Beijing) region is aws-cn.

[awscli](https://github.com/aws/aws-cli/blob/1.11.139/awscli/customizations/emr/createdefaultroles.py)
TL;DR
```
def _get_policy_arn_suffix(region):
  region_string = region.lower()
  if region_string.startswith("cn-"):
   return "aws-cn"
  elif region_string.startswith("us-gov"):
    return "aws-us-gov"
  else:
    return "aws"
```

Use the `AnalyticsConfigurations` property to configure an analysis filter for an Amazon S3 bucket.

issue_title: Glob warnings should be target-level, not individual-glob-level
issue_body:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/5769 introduced warnings for globs that don't match anything.

However this check is at the individual glob level, not the target level. In other words, if you have multiple globs in a target, every one has to match something separately. 

For example, the default globs for python_tests is ('test_*.py', '*_test.py'), so that shops can pick whether they prefer test_foo.py or foo_test.py as their idiom. But now you'll get a warning on whichever one you didn't pick...

issue_title: Feature proposal: register fixtures for any 'step_impl'
issue_body:
# Prerequisites
I am adding behave-django to an old-ish project, which provides many json-fixtures. So I am working with various fixtures, and many different steps. As fixtures are loaded in ```environment.py```, knowing which fixtures are loaded for a given ```step_impl``` is not always transparent - at least for me. I have therefore developed a decorator for fixtures, which can be applied to any ```step_impl```

The decorator accepts a list of strings that are then loaded in the ```before_scenario``` in the ```environment.py```. As I prefer callables to json-fixtures, the decorator also accepts paths to callables.

# Example
This is a step:
`@fixtures('languages.json', 'behave.fixtures.pages.wagtail_page')`
`@when('someone does something')`
`def step_impl(context):`
`    ...`

In the ```environment.py``` - ```before_scenario```, callables for all steps of given scenario are executed, and all other files are appended to ```context.fixtures```

# Question
So - please let me know in case you'd like me to provide a pull request, or if you consider this too specific for my project. Thanks a lot for your efforts!

issue_title: Response futures appear to be strings?
issue_body:
```
from dwave.system.samplers import DWaveSampler

sampler = DWaveSampler(profile='BAY4')

h = {}
J = {(0, 4): -1}

response = sampler.sample_ising(h, J, num_reads=10)
print response.done()
```
yields
```
AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'done'
```
If I print `response._futures` right after creating the response, I get 
```
{'ignore_extra_keys': True, 'samples_key': 'samples', 'futures': (<dwave.cloud.computation.Future object at 0x7f31793d8110>,), 'data_vector_keys': {'energies': 'energy', 'num_occurrences': 'num_occurrences'}, 'active_variables': [0, 4], 'variable_labels': [0, 4], 'info_keys': {'timing': 'timing'}}
```
Is it possible `response._futures` is temporarily overwritten somewhere? Apparently yes, `response._futures` can hold something else than futures: at https://github.com/dwavesystems/dimod/blob/master/dimod/response.py#L561, it gets populated with a bunch of stuff, including non-futures.

So `response._futures` can be different things at different stages of the response ... :-1:

During the course of embedding, the algorithm holds a rich embedding object that contains connectivity information for chains -- couplers that are used to make chains, and couplers that are used for chain interactions.  That information is currently being thrown away, only to be reconstructed by the user at a later stage.  It may be nice to handle this a little better; returning and accepting richer embedding objects  that contain full or partial connectivity information.

issue_title: Add license upload API in storops
issue_body:
here is the sample from Unity guide:

```
<!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.01 Transitional//EN">
<html>
 <body>
   <form enctype="multipart/form-data" method="post" action="https://<IP_address>/upload/license">
     <input type="file" name="filename>"/> 
      <input type="submit"/> 
    </form>
 </body>
</html>
```
A successful upload request returns 200 OK HTTP status code. If the request does not succeed, the server returns a 4nn or 5nn HTTP status code in the response header and a message entity in the response body.

It would be good to get the build to be green.

Should we separate out the failing tests into a folder where we keep failing tests (e.g. files that are known not to don't dected correctly), but only run these tests _exceptionally_  - e.g. during development testing with a flag?  

They could be aspirational targets to detect successfully. :)

issue_title: Using dot notation on returned rows
issue_body:
It would be handy if the cursor's `fetch()` functions returned something like named tuples so that the fields can be accessed using dot notation rather than having to refer to them by index.

```python
cursor.execute("select first_name, last_name from person")
row = cursor.fetchone()
print('Hello {}'.format(row[0]))          # this currently works
print('Hello {}'.format(row.first_name))  # this is not yet possible
```

py-rlp uses `develop` as it's primary development branch.  This is inline with all of the *old* ethereum python codebases, but incongruent with all of the *newer* python codebases.

### How can it be fixed

I propose we change the primary branch to be `master`.

issue_title: Permit bytes addresses for programmatic API usage
issue_body:
Update Web3.py to accept length=20 `bytes` values as an ABI address. This will be the only format for addresses that Web3.py accepts without a *checksum*.

- The `pythonic_middleware` and `abi_middleware` will need to be updated to handle this.
- The code used for contract parameter validation will need to be updated.
- The code related to filters may need to be changed too.
- Tests need to be written for contract and base API (`web3.eth.getBalance`, etc) use cases.
- Documentation needs to be updated to indicate that raw bytes addresses are accepted.

also see #883 

The implementation should aim to be as simple as possible, potentially normalizing to a common address format using a middleware or something rather than adjusting every API to support this new format.  Use your discretion on whether this is the right approach.

### What was wrong?

Documentation is currently all README based

### How can it be fixed?

Convert documentation to be Sphinx based and host on readthedocs.

issue_title: conda environments should set PIP_CONFIG_FILE to /dev/null
issue_body:
My `~/.config/pip/pip.conf` contains the lines
```
[install]
user = true
```
so that when working with the system python, `pip install foo` does a `--user` install (I never want to do a system-wide install, my linux package manager is there for that).  (See https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/user_guide/#config-file.)

But when in a conda env, this doesn't make much sense -- you just want to install into the environment.  Two ways to do this would be either to set `PIP_CONFIG_FILE` to `/dev/null` or possibly provide a `pip.conf` which undoes such changes in the environment itself as the environment config file is read last (this seems harder to do -- you'd probably want to undo all possible customizations, which seems awkward).

The GFF parser splits the attributes field on ; followed by space ("; "), but strictly, only the ';' is a record separator. Needs better parsing to also make sure not to split on ; enclosed by quotes.

The [documentation](http://pysam.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#pysam.AlignmentFile.pileup) for `pysam.AlignmentFile.pileup` mentions a "pass" stepper option, that doesn't seem to be implemented.

<https://github.com/pysam-developers/pysam/blob/master/pysam/calignmentfile.pyx#L906-L908>
and
<https://github.com/pysam-developers/pysam/blob/master/pysam/calignmentfile.pyx#L1907-L1921>

issue_title: Incorrect element identified in logs when source: name is missing from an age filter
issue_body:
With debug logging enabled, if you omit the `source: name` from an age filter on an alias action, the log points you to the timestring element:

```
Bad Value: "%Y.%m.%d", extra keys not allowed @ data['timestring']. Check configuration file.. Check configuration file.
```

Rather than pointing out the missing, required `source` setting for the age filter.

issue_title: When using logstash log format, all output should respect that
issue_body:
Currently, there appears to be output that doesn't respect `--logformat logstash` — for example, if I run:

`curator --logformat logstash delete indices --older-than 10 --time-unit days --timestring %Y.%m.%d`

and there aren't any indices older than 10 days, I get the "Job starting" and then the "No indices matched provided args" lines output in properly-formatted logstash JSON, but then I get the "No indices matched provided args" output AGAIN as plain text.

This means that if I pipe the curator output to logstash, the JSON-formatted lines get consumed properly, but the plain-text one causes logstash to hiccup and report a JSON parse failure.

issue_title: Feature request: a special "<text>" value for the hooks.yaml "files" field
issue_body:
Hi,

I had to implement this kind of "binary-files-filtering-out" today:
https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/pre_commit_hooks/utils.py#L10

So I made a quick overview of the existing checkers, and I found only 2 that are targeting **any** text file: [end-of-file-fixer](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/hooks.yaml#L76) and [trailing-whitespaces](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/hooks.yaml#L106). Both use a long list of known text files suffixes.

If ever you think this use case could come in handy, I'd suggest to provide a magic value like `<any text file>` to the `files` parameter in _hooks.yaml_, so that hooks developers don't have to re-implement this filtering each time.

You can freely re-use the code I mentioned above if it comes handy to you.

Also, sorry for the 3-issues-opened-in-a-row :) I got really excited by this tool and ideas for improvements just flowed in ! But they are really just humble features suggestions.

Cheers

issue_title: Google Internet Authority G2 and Equifax Secure Certificate Authority not included?
issue_body:
First of all I'd like you thank you for your efforts in working on a safer web. I do have a question on that safety though...

I'm using django-allauth and certifi since it's installed via dependencies. Django-allauth comes with a bunch of oauth "clients" and one them is Google. I learned that I'm not able to work with the Google OAuth flow when I'm using plain certifi as both **Google Internet Authority G2** and **Equifax Secure Certificate Authority** are not included in the bundle shipped with certifi.

I know (http://www.tenberge-ict.nl/solving-sslerror-at-accountsgooglelogincallback-on-django-allauth/)  what I did to get the software running, but I'm repeating this after each certifi update, which got me wondering. It looks like those CA's are not in the trust list. Why?

issue_title: nbgrader validation fails accessing external files
issue_body:
### Operating system
Windows 10 64 bit

### `nbgrader --version`
> Python version 3.6.1 |Anaconda 4.4.0 (64-bit)| (default, May 11 2017, 13:25:24) [MSC v.1900 64 bit (AMD64)]
> nbgrader version 0.5.3

### `jupyterhub --version` (if used with JupyterHub)
N/A

### Notebook - Help, About version info
> The version of the notebook server is 5.0.0

### Expected behavior
An exercise notebook reads an external file (e.g. data/data.txt) and autograding tests depend on reading this file. I edit the `source/` version before distributing.   All tests pass when running the notebook with Run All. I hit `Validate` and expect to see the same result.

### Actual behavior
If a notebook accesses an external file, validation will fail if Jupyter was launched from the root of the nbgrader directory (or anywhere except the specific directory where the notebook is). This is because Validate seems to set/(or be run from) the current directory to the launch dir of Jupyter.

This also happens with the `release` version (validation will fail even if the solution is correct, because the external files will not be accessible from the path `nbgrader validate` is run).

### Steps to reproduce the behavior
Create a new assignment.
Add any file (e.g. "data/data.txt"). It doesn't have to be in a subdirectory for this to happen.
Add an autograder answer and test like:

      ### BEGIN SOLUTION
      with open("data/data.txt") as f:
             contents = f.read()
      ### END SOLUTION

      # test
      # will fail because contents will not be defined
      assert(len(contents)>0)

Alternatively, just create an autograder test:
   
     import os
     raise(Exception(os.getcwd()))

Hit `Validate`
The current working directory will be the launch directory of Jupyter, and not the notebook directory. 

I can't see an obvious workaround for this. Apologies if I am missing something obvious.

Is there any way to paste text into the Jupyter terminal?   

On chrome I'm able to copy from the terminal no problem, but can't figure out how to get text in.

The terminal is quite handy, but being unable to paste in snippets of text makes it difficult to execute larger commands.  I may just not have been able to figure out how to do it.

issue_title: Allow notebooks to automatically load custom Mathjax configuration, on a per-notebook basis
issue_body:
I would like to be able to set a notebook metadata value to tell it to load a particular Mathjax configuration (in order to set up custom latex macros).  I think that being able to tell it to load a .js file would be sufficient.

I know that I can modify custom.js, but that would affect all of my notebooks and would not affect the desired notebook for other users.

I know that I can define latex macros in markdown cells, but this has to be done in every notebook and breaks when converting to latex/PDF.

I would be willing to write an nbextension to do this, if that's the only way.

I know that it is possible to use a cell above with markdown for labeling but this gets messy very quickly if cells are moved around or if new cells are being created.  Would it be possible to have an option for setting a name/label for each cell in future versions?  I've been doing one of two things: (1) creating a markdown code above; and (2) commenting the top of code.  The first method has some inconveniences as I mentioned above and the 2nd blends in with the rest of the code unless the comments are stylized with ascii characters surrounding them.  

This would make notebooks look very clean and would help workflow in general if this was an option.  

Just a suggestion, but I feel that it would be useful for the community. Thoughts?

issue_title: Incorrectly giving a dict as a ListType of ModelTypes does not raise a ModelValidationError
issue_body:
Apologies if this was already known or fixed in the dev branch, I took a quick search through the issues and didn't find anything that seemed to match.

Using:
```console
$ pip freeze | grep schematics
schematics==1.1.1
$ python --version
Python 2.7.10
```

And the following classes:
```python
from schematics.models import Model
from schematics.types.compound import ListType, ModelType
from schematics.types import StringType

class Child(Model):
    foo = StringType()

class Parent(Model):
    children = ListType(ModelType(Child))
```

This works as expected:
```
>>> Parent({'children': [{'foo': 'bar'}]})
```

This raises a `ModelValidationError` as expected
```
>>> Parent({'children': 'invalid'})
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 12, in <module>
    Parent({'children': 'invalid'})
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/models.py", line 242, in __init__
    self._data = self.convert(raw_data, strict=strict, mapping=deserialize_mapping)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/models.py", line 289, in convert
    return convert(self.__class__, raw_data, **kw)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/transforms.py", line 419, in convert
    partial=partial, strict=strict, mapping=mapping)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/transforms.py", line 119, in import_loop
    raise ModelConversionError(errors, data)
schematics.exceptions.ModelConversionError: {'children': [u'Please use a mapping for this field or Child instance instead of str.']}
```

This does not raise a `ModelValidationError` as I would expect:
```
>>> Parent({'children': {'foo': 'bar'}})
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 13, in <module>
    Parent({'children': {'foo': 'bar'}})
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/models.py", line 242, in __init__
    self._data = self.convert(raw_data, strict=strict, mapping=deserialize_mapping)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/models.py", line 289, in convert
    return convert(self.__class__, raw_data, **kw)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/transforms.py", line 419, in convert
    partial=partial, strict=strict, mapping=mapping)

    raw_value = field_converter(field, raw_value)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/transforms.py", line 416, in field_converter
    return field.to_native(value)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/types/compound.py", line 156, in to_native
    items = self._force_list(value)
  File "/tmp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/schematics/types/compound.py", line 149, in _force_list
    return [value[unicode(k)] for k in sorted(map(int, value.keys()))]
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'foo'
```

I'd be happy to try to submit a PR if this is a confirmed bug. Thanks!

```python
>>> from schematics.types import StringType
>>> from schematics.types.compound import ListType
>>> l = ListType(StringType)
>>> l.validate('foo')

```
